ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general dosa entry (FOODON_03302051); no specific egg dosa variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW covers dosa as a category; no egg-topped variant code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for thosai or dosa variants. None assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Egg Thosai is approximately one medium-sized pancake with egg, weighing around 120 g, typically served with coconut chutney and sambar.

nutrient_source.title: Egg Dosa / Thosai with Egg — nutrient profile derived from ICMR-NIN (Indian Food Composition Tables 2017) plain dosa values combined with one whole egg contribution, proportioned to 100 g composite
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition), cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database for Singapore-context thosai
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Plain dosa per 100 g from ICMR-NIN 2017 (energy 149 kcal, protein 3.7 g, fat 3.2 g, carbohydrate 26.0 g) was adapted by incorporating one whole egg (50 g) contribution into a 120 g serving, then back-calculated to per-100 g values. HPB Singapore Foodo thosai entry used for cross-validation of sodium and fibre. Values represent a reasonable composite estimate for egg thosai as served in Singapore.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of dosa (fermented rice-lentil pancake) — analogous estimate based on Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for egg thosai found. GI of 51 is based on plain dosa (fermented rice-lentil pancake) reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The addition of egg lowers the effective GI slightly due to protein and fat content; GI 51 is retained as a conservative estimate. GL calculated as (51 × 22.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.2 serving factor) / 100 = 13.7 per 120 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~210 mg per 100 g); higher when served with sambar or salty chutneys
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one piece and request low-sodium accompaniments, as the sodium content of egg thosai plus condiments can contribute meaningfully to the daily sodium target of under 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (22.5 g per 100 g) with low GI of 51; egg protein helps blunt postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: Egg thosai has a low GI (51) and the egg component adds protein that moderates postprandial glycaemia, making it a preferable choice over plain white rice or refined-flour alternatives, but portion control to one piece is advised for individuals managing blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol from egg (~95 mg per 100 g) and moderate saturated fat (1.5 g per 100 g); fermented batter provides beneficial lactic acid bacteria
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Egg thosai contains moderate dietary cholesterol from the egg; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to one piece per meal and pair with vegetable-based accompaniments rather than coconut chutney to reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg Thosai (also spelled Egg Dosa or Egg Dosai) is a South Indian fermented pancake made from a batter of ground rice and urad dal (black gram lentils) that has been fermented overnight, spread thin on a hot griddle, and topped with a beaten egg that is cooked into the surface. The result is a crispy-edged, slightly sour, savoury pancake with a soft egg layer in the centre. In Singapore, egg thosai is a staple of Indian hawker stalls and kopitiam Indian food counters, commonly served with coconut chutney, tomato chutney, and sambar (a lentil-vegetable soup). The fermentation process improves digestibility and increases B-vitamin content. Nutritionally, egg thosai provides moderate carbohydrates (~22.5 g per 100 g) from the rice-lentil base, meaningful protein (~6.8 g per 100 g) from both the lentil batter and the egg, and a low glycemic index of approximately 51, making it a more glycaemically favourable grain-based option than white bread or plain rice. The egg contributes cholesterol (~95 mg per 100 g) and fat-soluble vitamins including vitamin A and vitamin D. Sodium is moderate (~210 mg per 100 g) but can increase significantly with sambar and chutney accompaniments. Egg thosai is naturally gluten-free and suitable for lacto-ovo vegetarians and halal diets. It is a culturally significant breakfast and tiffin item across Tamil Nadu, Sri Lanka, Malaysia, and Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in egg thosai?
Is egg dosa good for diabetics?
What is the GI of dosa?
Is egg thosai suitable for weight loss?
Can I eat egg thosai if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact egg thosai entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN as a single composite record. Values were adapted from ICMR-NIN 2017 plain dosa per-100 g data combined with a proportioned whole-egg contribution, back-calculated to per-100 g. Cross-validated against HPB Foodo thosai entries. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain dosa. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation methodology is transparent and the source data are from approved primary sources.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted estimates: plain dosa base from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017, egg contribution calculated from USDA FoodData Central whole egg data (FDC ID 748967), combined into a 120 g serving (approximately 80 g dosa batter cooked + 40 g egg) and back-calculated to per-100 g. Vitamin C is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN; neither rice-lentil batter nor cooked egg contributes meaningful vitamin C). Trans fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero; no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation). Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and all micronutrients marked with positive values are estimated from component-level ICMR-NIN and USDA data. Fields marked -1 are not applicable sentinel values: none remain in this record as all fields have been estimated or confirmed. GI of 51 is an analogous estimate based on plain dosa from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008). GL per serving = (51 × 22.5 / 100) × 1.2 = 13.77, rounded to 13.7. Cuisine is classified as Indian (South Indian origin) rather than Malaysian, as thosai is a Tamil/South Indian dish served widely in Singapore and Malaysia. The field survey source listing under Malaysian cuisine reflects the restaurant context rather than the dish's culinary origin.
